title: Smart retrieval + infra discovery - Nimbus Retail
preview: Discovery focused on embeddings + reranking architecture for Nimbus Retail search. Customer currently uses BM25 + in-house vector store; main concerns are throughput at peak shopping events (QPS), embedding dimensionality tradeoffs, hybrid search latency, and options for hosted vs dedicated vs private deployments (VPC). Agreed to run a small throughput pilot and share security docs and pricing.

title: Activity ledger export + KMS onboarding - BlueHarbor Labs
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date/Time: 2025-08-19 16:00 UTC
- Duration: ~48 minutes
- Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Samuel Ortiz (BlueHarbor Head of IT), Maya Lin (BlueHarbor Security Architect), Evelyn Park (BlueHarbor Compliance), Jae Kim (BlueHarbor DevOps), Noah Reed (BlueHarbor Legal)

Summary:
Quick orientation for BlueHarbor on what Redwood captures in admin/activity telemetry, the export paths (push to S3, streaming webhook, syslog/third-party SIEM), and how Customer-Managed Keys (CMEK)/KMS integration works. Also covered retention options, immutable/offsite archives, a few SOC2 artifact asks, and a short demo of an event export sample.

Topics:
- Event types & schema (who/what/when/where)
- Export options (S3, streaming, connectors)
- Encryption at rest/in transit + CMEK/HSM options
- Retention, legal hold, immutable archives
- RBAC/SSO tie-ins and audit correlation
- Next steps: sample manifests, test key, pricing impact

Transcript:
00:00 Jordan Blake: hey everybody, thanks for joining, i'm Jordan from Redwood, i'll kick us off, real quick agenda, why we're here is to make sure your legal and security folks have the right detail for the admin ledger export and the KMS plan.
00:14 Samuel Ortiz: thanks Jordan, yep, we just want to nail down exactly what events you capture - we have auditors asking for full admin trails, not just high level.
00:24 Priya Nair: great question, samuel. at a high level Redwood logs identity events, admin actions, model lifecycle events, routing changes, and system-level API calls. examples: user_login, saml_login (sometimes transcribed as \"sam L\"), api_key_create/delete, permission_change, model_rollout, route_update, and also runtime errors with request ids so you can trace back.
00:46 Maya Lin: how granular are the permission_change events? e.g., if someone changes a role binding, do we get old/new role values and who initiated it?
00:55 Priya Nair: yes - we capture actor, timestamp, resource, old_value, new_value. it's all put into the event payload as JSON; the only caveat is PII in custom metadata is scrubbed per your settings, we can add field-level redaction if needed. sometimes the transcription will call it K V cache when we mention KV cache, pronouncing letters.
01:15 Jae Kim: on the export formats - we prefer parquet because it's smaller and easier to ingest into our lake. can you output parquet gzipped?
01:24 Jordan Blake: we support jsonl, newline-delimited json, and parquet output. parquet + gzip is a common option for large volumes. we stream daily batches or near-real-time depending on your account tier; dedicated customers can get lower-latency streams.
01:38 Maya Lin: is that via S3? we need to keep data in-region for EU/GDPR reasons, want to make sure residency holds.
01:46 Priya Nair: yes you can choose the export target. we write directly to customer S3 buckets (S3-compatible endpoints), or we can push events to an HTTPS endpoint or syslog for SIEM ingestion. for residency, we can route exports through region-specific ingress and will only write to buckets in that region if you require it.
02:04 Noah Reed: on the legal side, we need to understand immutability for legal holds - can you prevent deletion of exported logs for a retention window?
02:12 Jordan Blake: we don't delete what you export. if you use our offsite immutable store add-on we write an append-only object store and we support S3 Object Lock (governance/compliance mode) and legal hold flags.
02:26 Evelyn Park: what about retention defaults in Redwood itself? our compliance baseline requires 365 days for admin trail.
02:34 Priya Nair: default for hosted accounts is 90 days of full fidelity in the platform UI and raw exports can be kept per your config. for enterprise add-ons we offer 365 or custom retention windows, plus archive-only cold storage for 7+ years. there is a cost delta which Jordan will send.
02:55 Samuel Ortiz: and encryption - we need CMEK with HSM backed KMS. can you integrate with our cloud KMS?
03:03 Priya Nair: yes we support customer-managed keys. we call it CMEK. Redwood can encrypt exported objects using your key provider (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP KMS) or a supported HSM-backed KMS. we also support envelope encryption for downstream services.
03:18 Jae Kim: is key rotation supported without breaking decryption of archives?
03:22 Priya Nair: yes we have a key-rotation scheme that retains prior key material for a decrypt window - object metadata includes the key version used. we can also do double-encryption patterns on top of that if you require. small note: some transcripts show \"envelope enc--\" cut off, but that's just the ASR.
03:39 Maya Lin: we want event integrity - chain-of-custody, something like signed event blobs or HMACs so we can verify there was no tampering after export.
03:47 Jordan Blake: we offer optional signed manifests. each batch has a manifest.json with a SHA256 digest and optional PKI signing. for higher assurance we can add Merkle-tree style proofs for a ledger. that's an advanced feature we can enable for compliance customers.
04:10 Samuel Ortiz: nice. about the real-time streaming, does that include the full event payload? and what's the latency?
04:18 Priya Nair: streaming can be near real-time; for hosted API customers, 1-5s typical, enterprise with dedicated capacity can do sub-second depending on routing. yes the full event payload is pushed; you choose whether it contains sensitive custom metadata or that metadata is masked.
04:33 Noah Reed: are audit logs available in human readable formats for SOC auditors? sometimes they want CSV/Excel extracts with friendly cols.
04:40 Jordan Blake: we can generate CSV extracts from the JSON payload and provide a PDF-friendly summary export. for audits we also provide a compliance package: sample events, retention policy, evidence trail, and supporting docs for SOC2/ISO controls.
04:56 Maya Lin: how do we correlate these admin events with application-level logs? we need a common request id between Redwood calls and our services.
05:04 Priya Nair: every user-facing request and admin action has a request_id and a trace_id. we recommend you propagate the trace id in your downstream systems; Redwood's exports include the request_id field so you can join on it. in practice some of the ASR got \"trace eye D\" but that's fine, the field is Trace ID.
05:20 Samuel Ortiz: any limitations on retention when using CMEK? like, are encrypted archived objects still searchable?
05:26 Priya Nair: you can search metadata stored in plaintext indexes; we also support encrypted field indexing for some fields but full-text search over encrypted blobs is not supported. to search archives we typically keep an index layer (search index) in plaintext that references the encrypted objects.
05:44 Jae Kim: operationally, do you provide an export manifest schema? we want to map fields to our data lake ingestion pipeline.
05:51 Priya Nair: yes, we have a canonical export schema v1 with examples for jsonl and parquet; i'll attach the schema and a sample manifest. manifest fields include batch_id, sequence_range, timestamp_min/max, checksum, compression, encryption_key_id, region.
06:15 Maya Lin: ok, show me a short demo of a sample event?
06:21 Jordan Blake: sure i will screen share, but i'll just read a sample since this is a transcript: {\"event_type\":\"permission_change\", \"actor\":\"samuel.ortiz@blueharbor.com\", \"timestamp\":\"2025-08-19T15:58:12Z\", \"resource\":\"/orgs/bhl/projects/prod\", \"old\":\"role:viewer\", \"new\":\"role:admin\", \"request_id\":\"req_9a2f5\"}  - and that would show up in jsonl or be converted to parquet fields. sometimes the system writes request id as req_9 A 2 F five in ASR but the eventual export is normalized.
06:54 Noah Reed: do you keep config snapshots? auditors want to see the state at a point in time when an event occurred.
07:00 Priya Nair: yes, we optionally include configuration snapshots in the manifest for any batch that contains config-change events. that way you can reconstruct state.
07:12 Samuel Ortiz: about ingest - can Redwood push to our SIEM directly? we run Splunk/Elastic.
07:19 Jordan Blake: we have connectors: Splunk HEC, Elastic ingest via HTTP, or you can pull from S3 once objects are landed. there's also a Kafka mirror option for streaming into your cluster.
07:30 Jae Kim: is there a VM/agent to push logs internal to our network? we are air-gapped in places.
07:35 Priya Nair: for private deployments we ship an on-prem connector that runs in your network and talks only to the control plane for auth; it can push to internal syslog collectors. if you're fully air-gapped we'd look at the on-prem package.
07:50 Maya Lin: regarding RBAC/SSO - how do you surface who had what role at time T? is that in the admin ledger?
07:57 Priya Nair: yes role assignments and the history are recorded. we keep role binding change events and snapshot diffs. you can query through our API or export them.
08:08 Samuel Ortiz: any gotchas with retention costs - e.g., incremental cost for high-volume event streams?
08:15 Jordan Blake: yes, retention and throughput both matter. high-volume real-time streams increase egress and S3 PUT costs. our pricing doc has a retention pricing delta and ingestion tier pricing; i'll share an example bill pro forma.
08:29 Evelyn Park: our compliance will want proof of deletion if something is removed - is there an audit trail for deletions?
08:35 Priya Nair: delete events are recorded as admin actions. however once you've exported and removed from your bucket that's governed by your bucket policy or object lock. within Redwood, we keep a delete_event record in the ledger that shows actor and timestamp.
08:50 Noah Reed: do you support WORM (write once read many) style immutable store?
08:55 Jordan Blake: yes, via S3 Object Lock in compliance mode or our immutable offsite. we can produce signed manifests and time-stamped attestations for the store.
09:08 Samuel Ortiz: for access controls to the exports, who can pull the S3 export? are there service principals?
09:15 Priya Nair: export writes to your bucket using an IAM role or pre-signed credentials that you control; we never hold secrets for long. you can scope the role to a specific prefix. access to the Redwood UI export config is role gated via RBAC and SSO (SAML/Okta/G-Suite). sometimes calls will say SAML as \"Sam L\" in the transcript, but it's the same.
09:34 Jae Kim: what if we want a read-only role for auditors that can only view manifests and not the payload?
09:39 Priya Nair: we support partial export content filtering and a read-only auditor role; you can redact sensitive fields or create a view that omits them.
09:48 Maya Lin: do you have retention-lifecycle policies we can tie to legal hold? like escalate to cold after X days but prevent deletion when legal hold is active?
09:56 Jordan Blake: yes lifecycle + legal hold integration. you can freeze a prefix with object lock or maintain a separate legal-hold archive bucket.
10:06 Samuel Ortiz: on the SOC2 artifacts - what exact items can you provide to prove controls around logging?
10:12 Jordan Blake: we can provide system diagrams, logging policy docs, a sample of the last 90 days exported event set (redacted), evidence of key rotation policies, access control lists, and your dedicated audit log for admin console access. we also provide our SOC2 report if NDA is in place.
10:29 Evelyn Park: we'll need crosswalk documentation so our auditors can map BlueHarbor controls to Redwood controls.
10:34 Jordan Blake: we have a compliance crosswalk template - i'll share that as part of the artifact bundle.
10:41 Priya Nair: quick note on schema evolution - when we change event schema we version it and the manifest includes schema_version so your ingestion pipeline can handle v1/v2 mapping.
10:52 Jae Kim: sample retention numbers: how much will 365 days at our expected volume cost vs the default 90?
10:58 Jordan Blake: i'll run a pro forma given your projected event qps. that's one of the next steps.
11:06 Maya Lin: are there any operations SLAs for export delivery? e.g., 99.9% delivery to S3, retries?
11:13 Priya Nair: we have delivery guarantees depending on tier. default is best-effort with exponential backoff and 24h retry window; enterprise add-on guarantees 99.9% and persistent retries plus alerting.
11:26 Samuel Ortiz: what about correlation with identity provider events? we use Okta - can you show who authenticated vs internal token usage?
11:33 Priya Nair: we ingest IdP events if you elect to forward them, and we can correlate SAML/Okta session IDs to the admin events. that way you can show whether an event was triggered via SSO or an API key.
11:46 Noah Reed: last question for now: does Redwood maintain its own audit logs showing who accessed the audit exports themselves?
11:52 Jordan Blake: yes - Redwood records access to the export configuration and the manifest download; those are captured in the audit trail and can be exported too.
12:02 Samuel Ortiz: thanks that was helpful. can we confirm three deliverables? schema + sample manifest, SOC2 artifact list, and a sample CMEK test plan.
12:10 Jordan Blake: yep those are the next steps. we'll follow up by email with attachments.

Action items (recap):
- Priya: attach canonical export schema, sample jsonl + parquet, manifest example (due 2025-08-22)
- Jordan: send SOC2/ISO artifact checklist and pricing delta for 365d retention (due 2025-08-21)
- Jae/Samuel: provision test KMS key and share access to test S3 bucket (due 2025-08-26)

End of call.

Agreed to run a 2-week pilot with a reserved small node pool and distributed load profile; Redwood to share pricing, dedicated topology diagram, and security questionnaire.

title: POC pass/fail matrix and reporting alignment
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-07-14 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~46 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Mina (Redwood SE), Priya (BrightLayer Head of ML), Tom (Infra Lead), Raj (Product)
-- Optional summary (auto-gen may be noisy) --
Summary: Align on POC acceptance tests: pass/fail thresholds for latency/throughput/error, quality acceptance (label F1/accuracy), reporting format (CSV + dashboard) and timeline for signoff. Action owners and next steps captured.

[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roundtable — who we got?
[00:06] Priya: Priya here, hi. Head of ML at BrightLayer. We're leading the use-case for summarization + triage of patient messages.
[00:11] Tom: Tom — infra, will own the env and network.
[00:13] Raj: Raj here for product, mainly success metrics and deployment gating.
[00:17] Jordan: Great. Mina, want to kick off with proposed pass/fail matrix?
[00:20] Mina: Sure. I shared a working spreadsheet, POC_benchmarks.xlsx, in the calendar — apologies if the link was weird in the invite. title: Pilot inference tuning weekly sync - TTFT / streaming / kernel choices
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-07-02 09:31 UTC
Duration: 45 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Samir Patel (Redwood SE), Maya Torres (Aperture CTO), Ethan Li (Aperture ML Eng), Priya Rao (Aperture SRE)

Summary:
Weekly pilot sync to review TTFT numbers, streaming behavior, sequence length distributions, and decide kernel selection for the next pilot iteration. Quick review of current infra (H100 reserved test nodes + one A100 fallback), discuss KV cache behavior and quantization trade-offs. Action items and next steps assigned.

Topics:
- TTFT baseline and observed p50/p95 regressions
- Streaming vs non-streaming for short-turn tasks
- Sequence length profiles and kernel selection (flash attention, flash4, cuBLAS fallbacks)
- KV cache behavior and prefix caching opportunities
- Instrumentation and traces to collect

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call - Jordan from Redwood, Samir SE, and from Aperture we have Maya, Ethan, Priya. Uh, quick goal for today is to look at the last run you did mid-week, the TTFT numbers with streaming on, and pick kernels for the canary.

[00:23] Maya Torres: Yeah hi, thanks. We ran the 300-sample test on Monday on the H100 nodes. The p50 looked decent, around 180ms, but p95 spiked to 620ms which we didn't expect.

[00:41] Samir Patel: ok, couple quick clarifying q's — are those numbers with streaming enabled and kv cache warm?

[00:49] Ethan Li: streaming on, KV cache cold on the first pass. We did a second pass with cache warm and p95 dropped to ~340ms, p50 stayed ~170. But the first-token TTFT (TTFT) for new conversations is the main KPI for the product, so that cold-cache spike matters.

[01:10] Jordan Blake: Right, TTFT is the priority — we want <250ms p95 for first-token ideally, I know the pilot SLO signed was 300ms p95. So the cold path hitting 620 is outside that.

[01:28] Priya Rao: To add, our workload has a high percentage of short sessions — like 6-12 tokens typical. So streaming overhead matters a lot.

[01:40] Samir Patel: ok that helps. Two big levers here: kernel selection for short seqs and batching/buffer warmup. For short sequences, flash attention kernels give a big perf boost on H100 for seq lengths <128; but we saw a jitter when Triton fallback to cuBLAS happened on some micro-ops.

[02:04] Maya Torres: Yeah we saw some logs that said fallback path triggered around certain sequence lengths, I think 96->128 boundary. The instrumentation is a bit noisy, some logs call it flash-4 and sometimes flash4a, — transcription error — so had to guess.

[02:20] Samir Patel: That's the transcription of the runtime message, yeah. There's two variants: flash_attention_v2 (fast) and fallback_gemm (reliable). In our runtime we prefer flash when seq <= 128 and using q4_k23 quantization profile; beyond that we use fallback. But switching thresholds can be tuned.

[02:43] Ethan Li: Is quantization causing the spike? We're running q4_k8 for the pilot to save mem. Might be interaction.

[02:52] Samir Patel: Possibly — lower-precision kernels can increase compute on some ops because of dequant/depack costs. For TTFT, we recommend keeping first-token path on a higher precision kernel (q8 or fp16) and using q4 for steady-state token generation. That means slight memory increase but improves first token latency.

[03:15] Jordan Blake: Actionable: for next run, try first-token in fp16 on H100 and rest in q4_k8. Samir can share the config.

[03:25] Maya Torres: ok. Also question about batching - our service receives bursts and we have an autoscale policy; sometimes a request hits a cold pool and batching pipeline hasn't started so queuing costs add TTFT.

[03:39] Priya Rao: yeah our infra uses small reserved pool plus burst instances, so we see 2 patterns: reserved pool warm (good p50/p95) and burst cold (bad TTFT).

[03:50] Samir Patel: Because of the burst pattern, we suggested prefix/KV caching at the CDN/proxy layer for predictable prompts. Also Redwood has a fast start buffer — a lightweight warmup instance that keeps KV ready for the first token for priority customers. We can enable that for Aperture during the pilot at small cost.

[04:12] Ethan Li: Is that the "fast-start" feature? If so what's the cost and memory footprint?

[04:18] Jordan Blake: It's a thin reserved worker that keeps the model loaded and a warmed KV cache for recent prompts; cost is marginal compared to a full worker. We'll put expected burn numbers in the spreadsheet.

[04:32] Maya Torres: That could help. Back to kernel fallbacks — we saw a couple runs on A100 had better stability vs H100 for our config, even if raw ops/sec was lower. Why would that be?

[04:45] Samir Patel: A100 kernels and the old cuBLAS paths are sometimes more consistent for certain mixed-precision profiles. H100 has newer flash kernels that are faster but can be brittle with edge sequence lengths or certain attention patterns. So A100 sometimes gives monotonic latency at slightly higher p50.

[05:04] Priya Rao: So recommendation: use H100 for heavy steady-state traffic and A100 as a fallback tier — but for TTFT SLO maybe pin the first-token to A100 or fp16 lane?

[05:17] Jordan Blake: Yes, that's plausible. Another option is explicit kernel-pin per model variant: small model variant (fast-first-token) on A100 fp16, high-throughput variant on H100 with flash kernels. We'll propose a canary with those two variants.

[05:33] Ethan Li: ok makes sense. Regarding instrumentation, we need better traces: p50/p95 token times, kernel hops when fallback happens, KV cache hit rate. Samir, you had a trace plan?

[05:45] Samir Patel: I do. I'll attach flamegraphs and a Triton timeline. What we'd like from Aperture is the raw request ids and timestamps for the p95 outliers so we can map to kernel logs. If you can share a few sample request ids (anonymized) we'll correlate.

[06:02] Maya Torres: we'll share a redacted list. Also question on streaming framing — for short interactions, streaming can increase TTFT if we wait to assemble tokens before sending. Are you suggesting true token-by-token streaming?

[06:18] Samir Patel: Yes, true token-by-token streaming reduces perceived latency; however it can add overhead if your client isn't optimized. For TTFT we recommend starting with streaming for first token and then falling back to chunked streaming for throughput. Also we can enable immediate headers so client starts rendering earlier.

[06:37] Ethan Li: Our client currently waits for the full chunk of 16 tokens before rendering — that's part of the perceived delay. We'll change to immediate render for next test.

[06:46] Jordan Blake: Good. Small wins also include lowering max_sequence for the first-token path so kernel selection is always in the <128 fast lane. For your workload with typically 6-12 tokens, that's safe.

[07:01] Priya Rao: one last infra question — when we tested with dedicated pool and autoscale, some cold workers were started on spot instances with different driver versions, causing inconsistent kernels. Can Redwood ensure driver/runtime consistency in the pilot?

[07:18] Samir Patel: For the pilot we'll lock the AMI/container runtime and GPU driver across reserved and burst. We'll also surface the runtime version in the dashboard so you can see if a mismatch occurred.

[07:30] Maya Torres: awesome. On the observability front, we want a small dashboard tile for "first-token TTFT" and another for "kernel fallback rate". Are those available out of the box?

[07:40] Jordan Blake: Yes we've got both. Samir will share the dashboard link and permissions; we'll give Aperture viewer access and a SE write role for debug traces.

[07:51] Ethan Li: Great. Quick question on the model — we are on the open-model-v2.1 checkpoint. Any kernel compatibility concerns?

[07:58] Samir Patel: v2.1 is fine — main issue is architecture (decoder-only vs long-context encoder) and tokenizers. No kernel changes required but sequence patterns (long prefixes) can push you into different kernel branches. We'll profile sample prefixes.

[08:18] Maya Torres: Cool. Let's go over the action items and dates quickly.

[08:25] Jordan Blake: Samir to upload flame graphs and Triton logs by 2025-07-06. Ethan to run the 1k TTFT streaming test on H100 using fp16 first-token config and post p50/p95 by 2025-07-09. Jordan to send kernel-selection spreadsheet and cost estimate by 2025-07-04. Aperture to provide 5 sample request IDs for outliers by 2025-07-03.

[09:01] Priya Rao: We'll do that. One more note — security / logging: do you persist raw prompts in any logs by default? We need assurance of redaction for PII.

[09:12] Jordan Blake: By default we redact sensitive fields in our logs for hosted pilot, and for private deployments you manage retention. I'll attach the security note and the SOC2 section to the follow-up.

[09:23] Maya Torres: Thanks. Any competitors to be aware of? We are also testing with Vertex AI for latency but Redwood's tooling looks more tailored for inference economics.

[09:35] Jordan Blake: Noted. We'll include a short comparison grid in the follow-up doc.

[09:42] Ethan Li: Ok thanks all.

[09:45] Jordan Blake: Thanks — we'll reconvene same time next week unless we decide to do a mid-week deep dive after the H100 run.

[09:52] Meeting end.

[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call - Jordan from Redwood, Samir SE, and from Aperture we have Maya, Ethan, Priya. Uh, quick goal for today is to look at the last run you did mid-week, the TTFT numbers with streaming on, and pick kernels for the canary.

[00:23] Maya Torres: Yeah hi, thanks. We ran the 300-sample test on Monday on the H100 nodes. The p50 looked decent, around 180ms, but p95 spiked to 620ms which we didn't expect.

[00:41] Samir Patel: ok, couple quick clarifying q's — are those numbers with streaming enabled and kv cache warm?

[00:49] Ethan Li: streaming on, KV cache cold on the first pass. We did a second pass with cache warm and p95 dropped to ~340ms, p50 stayed ~170. But the first-token TTFT (TTFT) for new conversations is the main KPI for the product, so that cold-cache spike matters.

[01:10] Jordan Blake: Right, TTFT is the priority — we want <250ms p95 for first-token ideally, I know the pilot SLO signed was 300ms p95. So the cold path hitting 620 is outside that.

[01:28] Priya Rao: To add, our workload has a high percentage of short sessions — like 6-12 tokens typical. So streaming overhead matters a lot.

[01:40] Samir Patel: ok that helps. Two big levers here: kernel selection for short seqs and batching/buffer warmup. For short sequences, flash attention kernels give a big perf boost on H100 for seq lengths <128; but we saw a jitter when Triton fallback to cuBLAS happened on some micro-ops.

[02:04] Maya Torres: Yeah we saw some logs that said fallback path triggered around certain sequence lengths, I think 96->128 boundary. The instrumentation is a bit noisy, some logs call it flash-4 and sometimes flash4a, — transcription error — so had to guess.

[02:20] Samir Patel: That's the transcription of the runtime message, yeah. There's two variants: flash_attention_v2 (fast) and fallback_gemm (reliable). In our runtime we prefer flash when seq <= 128 and using q4_k23 quantization profile; beyond that we use fallback. But switching thresholds can be tuned.

[02:43] Ethan Li: Is quantization causing the spike? We're running q4_k8 for the pilot to save mem. Might be interaction.

[02:52] Samir Patel: Possibly — lower-precision kernels can increase compute on some ops because of dequant/depack costs. For TTFT, we recommend keeping first-token path on a higher precision kernel (q8 or fp16) and using q4 for steady-state token generation. That means slight memory increase but improves first token latency.

[03:15] Jordan Blake: Actionable: for next run, try first-token in fp16 on H100 and rest in q4_k8. Samir can share the config.

[03:25] Maya Torres: ok. Also question about batching - our service receives bursts and we have an autoscale policy; sometimes a request hits a cold pool and batching pipeline hasn't started so queuing costs add TTFT.

[03:39] Priya Rao: yeah our infra uses small reserved pool plus burst instances, so we see 2 patterns: reserved pool warm (good p50/p95) and burst cold (bad TTFT).

[03:50] Samir Patel: Because of the burst pattern, we suggested prefix/KV caching at the CDN/proxy layer for predictable prompts. Also Redwood has a fast start buffer — a lightweight warmup instance that keeps KV ready for the first token for priority customers. We can enable that for Aperture during the pilot at small cost.

[04:12] Ethan Li: Is that the "fast-start" feature? If so what's the cost and memory footprint?

[04:18] Jordan Blake: It's a thin reserved worker that keeps the model loaded and a warmed KV cache for recent prompts; cost is marginal compared to a full worker. We'll put expected burn numbers in the spreadsheet.

[04:32] Maya Torres: That could help. Back to kernel fallbacks — we saw a couple runs on A100 had better stability vs H100 for our config, even if raw ops/sec was lower. Why would that be?

[04:45] Samir Patel: A100 kernels and the old cuBLAS paths are sometimes more consistent for certain mixed-precision profiles. H100 has newer flash kernels that are faster but can be brittle with edge sequence lengths or certain attention patterns. So A100 sometimes gives monotonic latency at slightly higher p50.

[05:04] Priya Rao: So recommendation: use H100 for heavy steady-state traffic and A100 as a fallback tier — but for TTFT SLO maybe pin the first-token to A100 or fp16 lane?

[05:17] Jordan Blake: Yes, that's plausible. Another option is explicit kernel-pin per model variant: small model variant (fast-first-token) on A100 fp16, high-throughput variant on H100 with flash kernels. We'll propose a canary with those two variants.

[05:33] Ethan Li: ok makes sense. Regarding instrumentation, we need better traces: p50/p95 token times, kernel hops when fallback happens, KV cache hit rate. Samir, you had a trace plan?

[05:45] Samir Patel: I do. I'll attach flamegraphs and a Triton timeline. What we'd like from Aperture is the raw request ids and timestamps for the p95 outliers so we can map to kernel logs. If you can share a few sample request ids (anonymized) we'll correlate.

[06:02] Maya Torres: we'll share a redacted list. Also question on streaming framing — for short interactions, streaming can increase TTFT if we wait to assemble tokens before sending. Are you suggesting true token-by-token streaming?

[06:18] Samir Patel: Yes, true token-by-token streaming reduces perceived latency; however it can add overhead if your client isn't optimized. For TTFT we recommend starting with streaming for first token and then falling back to chunked streaming for throughput. Also we can enable immediate headers so client starts rendering earlier.

[06:37] Ethan Li: Our client currently waits for the full chunk of 16 tokens before rendering — that's part of the perceived delay. We'll change to immediate render for next test.

[06:46] Jordan Blake: Good. Small wins also include lowering max_sequence for the first-token path so kernel selection is always in the <128 fast lane. For your workload with typically 6-12 tokens, that's safe.

[07:01] Priya Rao: one last infra question — when we tested with dedicated pool and autoscale, some cold workers were started on spot instances with different driver versions, causing inconsistent kernels. Can Redwood ensure driver/runtime consistency in the pilot?

[07:18] Samir Patel: For the pilot we'll lock the AMI/container runtime and GPU driver across reserved and burst. We'll also surface the runtime version in the dashboard so you can see if a mismatch occurred.

[07:30] Maya Torres: awesome. On the observability front, we want a small dashboard tile for "first-token TTFT" and another for "kernel fallback rate". Are those available out of the box?

[07:40] Jordan Blake: Yes we've got both. Samir will share the dashboard link and permissions; we'll give Aperture viewer access and a SE write role for debug traces.

[07:51] Ethan Li: Great. Quick question on the model — we are on the open-model-v2.1 checkpoint. Any kernel compatibility concerns?

[07:58] Samir Patel: v2.1 is fine — main issue is architecture (decoder-only vs long-context encoder) and tokenizers. No kernel changes required but sequence patterns (long prefixes) can push you into different kernel branches. We'll profile sample prefixes.

[08:18] Maya Torres: Cool. Let's go over the action items and dates quickly.

[08:25] Jordan Blake: Samir to upload flame graphs and Triton logs by 2025-07-06. Ethan to run the 1k TTFT streaming test on H100 using fp16 first-token config and post p50/p95 by 2025-07-09. Jordan to send kernel-selection spreadsheet and cost estimate by 2025-07-04. Aperture to provide 5 sample request IDs for outliers by 2025-07-03.

[09:01] Priya Rao: We'll do that. One more note — security / logging: do you persist raw prompts in any logs by default? We need assurance of redaction for PII.

[09:12] Jordan Blake: By default we redact sensitive fields in our logs for hosted pilot, and for private deployments you manage retention. I'll attach the security note and the SOC2 section to the follow-up.

[09:23] Maya Torres: Thanks. Any competitors to be aware of? We are also testing with Vertex AI for latency but Redwood's tooling looks more tailored for inference economics.

[09:35] Jordan Blake: Noted. We'll include a short comparison grid in the follow-up doc.

[09:42] Ethan Li: Ok thanks all.

[09:45] Jordan Blake: Thanks — we'll reconvene same time next week unless we decide to do a mid-week deep dive after the H100 run.

[09:52] Meeting end.

title: Dual-Lang SDK Handoff - CI Secrets & Quickstart
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2025-04-18 16:00 PDT
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Marcus Chen (TwinLeaf), Aisha Rodriguez (TwinLeaf), Tomás Alvarez (TwinLeaf)
Recording: auto-generated
Shared artifacts: Dual-lang quickstart repo (short link provided in chat), Redwood console demo deck (link), bench spreadsheet referenced: /drive/TwinLeaf-redwood-bench.xlsx

Summary:
Quick 1:1 handoff focused on SDK integration in two languages (Python + TypeScript), CI secrets best practices, environment bootstrapping, and a small perf sanity check between hosted and dedicated. Customer has PoC target in 6 weeks, wants CI examples and notes on private deployment constraints.

Topics:
- Quickstart walkthrough (Python + TS)
- Auth patterns and CI secret injection
- Env variables, local dev vs CI, and docker examples
- Performance expectations and batching knobs in Redwood Optimize
- Next steps: repo, small benchmark, private deployment follow-up

Transcript Body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining, I think we have about 50 minutes. Uh, quick intros? I'll kick off — Jordan Blake, AE at Redwood.
[00:10] Marcus: Hi, Marcus Chen, platform eng at TwinLeaf. Trying to get a clean CI + SDK path for our feature teams.
[00:16] Aisha: Aisha, ML eng, mostly model infra and embeddings pipelines.
[00:22] Tomás: Tomás here, SRE, will be the one looking at network & secrets.
[00:28] Priya: Priya Nair, solutions engineer on the infra team, I'll do the deep-dive on SDK and config.
[00:34] Jordan: Cool. High level agenda: run through the quickstart in both languages, show the auth patterns we recommend for CI, talk through environment setup and a brief mention of Redwood Optimize tuning for batching. Any additions?
[00:46] Marcus: Sounds good. Important for us: we want to avoid hard-coding keys, and we use GitHub Actions and HashiCorp Vault primarily. Also need to know when we should move from hosted to dedicated capacity.
[00:58] Priya: Got it — we'll show a CI secret snippet and also the small fallback pattern for when dedicated not available.
[01:05] Jordan: Quick housekeeping, shared repo link in chat: tinyurl.com/rlwd-dual-quickstart (note: not the final org repo).
[01:14] Priya: Alright, starting with Python. We'll open the sample app — it's intentionally minimal. Requirements: python 3.10+, pip install redwood-client (example name), set REDWOOD_API_KEY as env. Code snippet: import redwood; client = redwood.Client(api_key=os.getenv('REDWOOD_API_KEY'))... then generate.
[01:35] Aisha: Quick Q: do we have an official python package name? I saw redwood-sdk in docs.
[01:40] Jordan: There's a naming mismatch sometimes in notes — current pip is redwood-inference-client in org packages, but the quickstart uses a stable alias for clarity. We'll include exact pip name in the README.
[01:52] Priya: For TypeScript, similar pattern: npm i @redwood/inference, use process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY, and we demo both fetch-based and the official Node SDK that supports streaming.
[02:05] Marcus: When you say streaming, do you mean SSE or web socket?
[02:09] Priya: SSE/HTTP chunked responses for the hosted API; Node SDK wraps the stream into an async iterator. We'll show the small code snippet.
[02:20] Jordan: Let's jump to CI secrets and avoid the common pitfalls. Tomás, you said you use Vault?
[02:26] Tomás: Yeah Vault for production secrets, but GH Actions for lower envs. We want a pattern that plays nice with both.
[02:33] Priya: Recommended: don't check in keys, use ephemeral secrets if possible. For GH Actions we show an example with secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY and a small wrapper step to inject into the job env. For Vault we show a short script to pull and export REDWOOD_API_KEY for the runner.
[02:50] Jordan: Example GH Actions snippet in the repo:
- name: Set REDWOOD key
  env:
    REDWOOD_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.REDWOOD_API_KEY }}
  run: echo 'REDWOOD_API_KEY set'

[03:05] Aisha: For local dev, should engineers create scoped keys per developer or use a shared dev key?
[03:12] Priya: We usually recommend scoped dev keys per engineer when possible; for orgs that want lower friction, a shared dev key with IP-based limits can work. Also, set low quotas on dev keys to limit abuse.
[03:26] Marcus: Ok, that's helpful. What about rate limits and batching? Our prod QPS target is about 60 requests/sec but most are short prompts with embeddings and small chat.
[03:36] Priya: That maps well — Redwood Optimize has batching suggestions: you can set preferred max_batch_tokens and preferred latency targets. For 60 QPS of short prompts, continuous batching with small max latency (20-50ms) is the usual starting point. We'll also include a recommended initial config in the repo.
[03:54] Tomás: Is there an easy way to toggle between hosted and dedicated endpoints? If we reserve Dedicated later, do we need to change code?
[04:01] Jordan: No code change usually — endpoint abstraction in SDK supports routing by policy. You can set a config variable or environment flag and the SDK will use a different base_url. We show that in the sample with REDWOOD_ENV=hosted|dedicated.
[04:14] Marcus: Nice. Anyone else using SSO/key rotation? We will need to rotate keys programmatically.
[04:19] Priya: Yes, we recommend short-lived tokens for high-security envs and integrate with your CI to fetch tokens. We'll link the token exchange doc in the repo.
[04:28] Jordan: Quick demo: I'll share my screen and walk the Python quickstart run locally so you can see logs and the env pattern.
[04:36] [screen share noise]
[04:42] Priya: You might see a slight transcription glitch but colors are fine — code run: python main.py -> calls the API and prints output. Note: logs redact the API key by default in SDK.
[04:58] Aisha: We noticed earlier that Redwood Optimize produces config recs — is that automatic or do we have to run something?
[05:05] Priya: Optimize can run in two modes: automatic suggestion based on observed workload (if you opt in), or manual evaluation where you upload a small trace and we return recommendations. For PoC, manual is faster — send over 1000 requests sample and we return batching + quantization suggestions.
[05:23] Marcus: Great. We have some custom LLMs too — can you host our model in Dedicated or must it be in Private?
[05:30] Jordan: We support serving customer-owned models in Redwood Dedicated and in Redwood Private deployments. For on-prem or air-gapped, Private is the route. Dedicated can host private models if they are uploaded to a secure tenant, but if you have strict data residency or no egress, Private is better. We'll need to discuss network topology.
[05:46] Tomás: On that note, what kind of KMS integrations do you support? We use AWS KMS and HashiCorp HSM in some regions.
[05:55] Priya: AWS KMS, Google KMS, and customer-managed HSMs via our Private offering — we'll attach the security docs, but in short, keys can be customer-managed for Private and Dedicated with appropriate RBAC.
[06:10] Jordan: Returning to quickstart, I want to highlight CI snippet for tests: run a smoke test using a low-cost model and mock tokens or a test key to avoid billing surprises. Example in repo includes a 'smoke' workflow.
[06:26] Aisha: Do you have a test stub or a local emulator?
[06:30] Priya: We have a local offline mode that returns canned responses for dev tests; it's not a full emulator but useful for CI unit tests. Note: it's not suitable for perf benchmarks.
[06:40] Marcus: We'll use that for unit tests but we'll need the real hosted endpoint for our integration tests.
[06:44] Jordan: Let's run the Node quickstart briefly. The Node sample shows streaming and structured output patterns (JSON schema enforcement).
[06:51] [background typing]
[07:04] Priya: Node snippet: const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY }); await client.chat.stream(...). The stream yields tokens and you can pipe directly to the browser or server-sent events.
[07:18] Marcus: Any gotchas around Node version or ESM vs CJS?
[07:22] Priya: We support both, but streaming requires Node 18+ for stable fetch/stream behavior. We call that out in the README.
[07:33] Aisha: Could you talk about embeddings flow? We plan to store vectors in our vector DB and want guidance on normalization and tokenization.
[07:40] Priya: For embeddings, we suggest batching vector calls, set a max_batch_size and lower latency threshold. Normalize text by lowercasing and stripping certain punctuation unless you need exact case. We'll include a suggested preproc snippet in the repo.
[07:56] Jordan: Ok, quick pivot to perf — Priya, can you show the small benchmark config you mentioned?
[08:02] Priya: Sure, it's a script in tools/bench that calls the same prompt payload 100 times with concurrency 8, captures p50/p95, and posts results as CSV. For the PoC, we'll run this on hosted and on a small dedicated node to compare. We'll send the results.
[08:20] Tomás: How long does it take to spin up a dedicated pool for a small PoC?
[08:25] Jordan: Depends on capacity, but typically 24-72 hours once the contract is in place. For immediate testing we do hosted with reserved burst credits or short-term dedicated trial if capacity allows.
[08:38] Marcus: Could you outline the cost delta? Rough per-token, per-hour guidance?
[08:44] Jordan: We'll send a tailored cost estimate in the follow-up. Quick rule: dedicated amortizes GPU cost for sustained high QPS; hosted is usually cheaper short-term for variable workloads. Priya will attach a simple spreadsheet with assumptions.
[08:59] [minor crosstalk]
[09:05] Aisha: Security question — audit logs for API calls and data residency controls?
[09:10] Priya: Audit logs available in Console, retains events per your plan. For data residency, Private supports on-prem and region pinning. We'll include SOC2 & ISO attachments.
[09:23] Marcus: Perfect. We need the security pack for our compliance team.
[09:27] Jordan: We'll push the security docs to the shared link after the call.
[09:33] [silence then typing]
[09:48] Priya: Recap of the technical bits we covered: Python + TS quickstart, CI secret patterns (GH Actions + Vault), dev vs CI keys, local offline mode for unit tests, small perf bench script, notes on hosted vs dedicated and private.
[09:59] Marcus: That's comprehensive. For next steps, we'll want the quickstart repo, the GH Actions snippet, and a small benchmark run from you.
[10:07] Jordan: Yep, action items recorded. Any remaining questions?
[10:12] Tomás: One last one: any recommended Redis or KV cache patterns for prompting large context windows?
[10:18] Priya: For KV cache we recommend prefix caching and TTLs keyed by conversation session. Avoid caching model outputs unless deterministic. We'll include a short pattern doc.
[10:28] Aisha: Also, if we choose quantized models later, is there any SDK change?
[10:32] Priya: No SDK change, just model selection and possibly different latency profiles; Optimize will suggest quantization when it's safe for your evals.
[10:40] Marcus: Great. Thanks everyone.
[10:44] Jordan: Thanks TwinLeaf. We'll send the repo, CI snippets, and run the 100-request bench and follow up. Meeting over.

End of transcript.

Notes on transcript quality: Auto-transcription had occasional name/case issues (Tomás transcribed as Tomas sometimes), and some product names abbreviated. There are a couple of places where audio overlapped and words were dropped.

title: OTel tracing + token metrics: KV/batching/quantization performance deep dive with Zenith Health
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-04-07
Start: 15:00 PT
Duration: ~62 minutes
Attendees: Mira Patel (Redwood AE), Liam Chen (Redwood SE), Sofia Rivera (Redwood Solutions), Jordan Blake (Redwood Product), Aisha Gomez (Zenith Head of ML), Tom Hsu (Zenith DevOps), Rajiv Menon (Zenith CTO), Lea Park (Zenith Security)
Recording id: ff-20250407-zenith-otel-td-001

Auto-summary (auto-generated - may be sparse):
- Deep dive on OpenTelemetry (OTel) integration for token-level metrics and tracing.
- Discussion of KV cache effects on latency/throughput, batching strategies, quantization trade-offs.
- Pilot plan: Dedicated pool, VPC peering, run token-latency benchmarks, share dashboards and security docs.

Topics: OTel setup, token metrics, KV cache prefixing, batching windows, int8/int4 quantization latency, VPC peering, KMS/SOC2

Action items (captured in metadata): see action_items field

Transcript body:
[00:00] Mira: hey everyone, thanks for making time, uh quick intro — I'm Mira, AE at Redwood. we'll go through tracing and token metrics and then Liam will show some traces and perf examples.
[00:18] Aisha: thanks Mira, excited. we've got throughput pain during peak telehealth windows and need better observability into per-token latencies.
[00:30] Liam: hi all. real quick agenda: 1) OTel collector + spans we emit; 2) token-level metrics and prometheus exposition; 3) runtime knobs (kv cache, batching, quantization); 4) proposed pilot. sound ok?
[00:48] Tom: yep sounds good — also will be watching network/VPC constraints and egress.
[00:55] Rajiv: and security — Lea will flag anything on encryption or key mgmt.

[02:10] Liam: okay, first — OpenTelemetry. Redwood emits span-level traces for request lifecycle: ingress, routing, model selection, runtime token loop, and egress. note: in some transcribes collectors the name got munged (we saw "redwoo-d" in earlier logs) — so mapping service names is important.
[02:40] Mira: we also add attributes for model.version, backend_node, batch_id, and cache_hit = true/false. these are available as span attributes via OTel.
[03:05] Aisha: quick Q — does the token-level metric mean you actually emit per-token timings? isn't that a lot of cardinality?
[03:20] Liam: yes, we emit per-token duration samples but we bucket and aggregate. the approach: per-request histogram of per-token latency and a summary gauge for p50/p95/p99 across ingestion windows. we avoid high-cardinality by not tagging tokens themselves, only position and stage, e.g., token_index, generation_stage.
[04:05] Tom: so how does that map to Prometheus? prometheus hates high-cardinality.
[04:17] Liam: we expose two surfaces: 1) an OTLP exporter for traces (Jaeger/Grafana Tempo/Lightstep) so you can see full spans, and 2) metrics suitable for Prometheus — histograms with buckets for token latency, labeled by model_family, quantization_profile, and cache_hit. labels intentionally limited.
[04:50] Aisha: can we see an example dashboard?
[04:55] Mira: yep, I'll share the dashboard JSON after the call — link: https://gist.github.com/redwood/zenith-otel-dashboard (note: sample link).

[06:05] Liam: demo time — pulling up a trace. (screen share noises) 
[06:20] Speaker 1: okay we see a trace where first tokens are slower because KB cache cold start, then subsequent tokens drop by ~40% latency.
[06:42] Liam: exact numbers — cold prefix where KV cache was empty: avg token latency ~28ms, warm cache avg ~16ms, end-to-end request latency improved by ~200ms for 256-token responses in this workload.
[07:05] Aisha: that's promising. is the KV cache the key-value attention cache? we're using it as prefix cache for common telehealth prompts.
[07:20] Liam: yes — our "KV cache" (spoken sometimes as kay-vee) has two modes for customers: local per-request KV (fast but ephemeral) and shared prefix cache for reserved capacity where common prompts are cached across requests. prefix cache needs extra memory and TTL tuning.
[07:50] Tom: what's the consistency model for that shared prefix cache if we update models?
[08:00] Jordan: we version the cache per model.version — when you rollout a new model we bump cache namespace so old entries aren't used. we also support invalidation APIs.

[09:15] Rajiv: ok batching — we'd like to aggregate many short requests to reduce cost but we have strict 200ms p95 latency SLO. how do you recommend balancing?
[09:30] Liam: two levers — batch size cap and batch timeout window. Redwood runtime uses adaptive batching: under low concurrency small windows, under high throughput it increases batch size. For strict p95 SLOs we recommend conservative timeout (e.g., 10ms) and smaller max_batch (e.g., 8) and enable prefix/KV cache to reduce compute per token.
[10:05] Aisha: we are interested in quantization — does int8/int4 change latency characteristics? we heard it sometimes increases per-token latency due to dequant overhead.
[10:20] Liam: good point. quantization trade-offs: int8 typically gives modest speedups and memory reduction with small accuracy hit, int4 is more aggressive and can have variable effects. On some GPUs int4 kernels are highly optimized so you see throughput gains; on others software dequant can add a small CPU overhead. We provide per-hardware recommendations in Optimize.
[11:00] Mira: note: our Optimize tool will suggest quantization profile by workload — we'll share a sample profiling report.

[11:35] Aisha: regarding traces, we want to tag traces to correlate with our internal request IDs — is there a header we pass through?
[11:45] Liam: yes, propagate traceparent and our x-request-id header. we also ingest your correlation id into spans. watch out for sampling — default sampling is adaptive; for deep debugging you can set sampling to 100% for pilot traffic.
[12:15] Tom: network hops — if we run private deployment in our VPC vs Redwood Dedicated in a peered VPC, how do observability flows differ?
[12:30] Mira: in Hosted mode metrics flow to Redwood telemetry endpoints; with Private (VPC/on-prem) you host the collector and forward to your observability stack — we provide collector config and dashboards. For Dedicated with VPC peering we support direct OTLP over gRPC to your OTEL collector inside your VPC.
[12:58] Lea: KMS — for telemetry do you send any PII in spans? we need to ensure PHI is not leaking in logs/traces.
[13:12] Liam: we never include raw user content in spans by default. token-level metrics are position and timing only; content is redacted. we can also apply field-level suppression in the collector config.

[14:00] Speaker 2: (mumbling) there's a little variation across backends — earlier we saw mis-attribution of spans when autoscaling triggered.
[14:20] Liam: yes, autoscale churn can fragment traces; recommended approach: use stable instance ids and pre-attach trace attributes like model_version and pool_id, also increase trace sampling during rollout windows.

[15:05] Aisha: concrete benchmark ask: we want a token-latency baseline for our top-50 prompts under both cold cache and warm cache, with int8 vs fp16. can Redwood produce that?
[15:20] Jordan: yes — we'll run baseline on Dedicated with same GPU family you plan to use, capture OTLP traces and prometheus metrics, and share CSV + dashboard.
[15:40] Tom: network egress — peering will reduce RTT but we need ip allowlist for the testing agent.
[15:52] Mira: we'll provide IP ranges and network playbook.

[17:10] (screen share — dashboard)
[17:30] Liam: showing token latency heatmap: x-axis token index, y-axis request id, color = ms. warm cache rows show consistent low ms bands, cold ones start high then drop. that's how you spot cache effectiveness.
[18:00] Aisha: can you compute effective cost-per-token delta when enabling cache? we want dollars saved if p95 drops by 30% for typical prompts.
[18:15] Jordan: yes we have cost-modeler calc, we'll run your sample set and produce delta.

[19:00] Speaker 3: note — Prometheus exporter exposes metrics like redwood_token_latency_bucket{model_family="gptx-small",quant_profile="int8",cache_hit="true"} and summary metrics redwood_token_latency_p95.
[19:25] Tom: so labels are limited to avoid cardinality spike. good.

[21:00] Liam: demo of a trace where batching latency tradeoff shows: one trace with larger batch has higher initial wait (queueing) but lower per-token GPU time, net trade depends on tokens-per-request.
[21:25] Aisha: so for short chats (<=64 tokens) batching hurts latency, for long generative responses batching wins.
[21:40] Liam: exactly. that's why adaptive batching with request size awareness is useful. we can also pin routes to low-latency dedicated pools where batching strategy is different.

[23:10] Mira: question for Zenith: what's your expected QPS during peak?
[23:20] Aisha: currently ~140 QPS sustained for small prompts during morning peak; spikes to ~420 QPS for brief windows.
[23:32] Jordan: that informs our Dedicated sizing — probably need 2-3 GPU pods with autoscale.

[25:00] Tom: about on-prem — if we run Redwood Private in our data center, do you still support the same tracing/metrics surface?
[25:12] Liam: yes, same interface — you run the control plane privately and the runtime emits OTLP to your collector. the only difference is where dashboards and long-term storage live.
[25:35] Lea: and audit logs for SOC2?
[25:40] Mira: we can retain audit logs at your retention window, and provide HMACed logs to satisfy integrity requirements. security pack will include examples.

[27:00] (brief cross talk)
[27:30] Aisha: what's the recommended sampling rate for pilot? want to be safe but not drown our tracing backend.
[27:40] Liam: start at 10-20% adaptive and bump to 100% for a short window when diagnosing performance regressions. we also support tail-based sampling rules in collector.

[29:00] Rajiv: any gotchas with quantization + batching + KV cache combined? like interactions we should watch for?
[29:18] Liam: yes — three main interactions: 1) quantization sometimes changes effective token latency per GPU kernel which can change batching optimal size; 2) KV cache reduces compute, which reduces batch formation pressure (so batching windows might need retuning); 3) cache invalidation across model rollouts can cause transient cold starts. Watch p50/p95 when toggling multiple knobs.

[31:05] Mire (sic): (speaker mislabel) oh sorry — a note that our runtime logs include a "cache_miss_rate" metric per prefix namespace, that'll help monitor freshness.

[32:00] (quiet)
[32:30] Liam: practical demo — we ran a 10k request synthetic on gpus in ded. Cold cache baseline int16: p95 token latency 35ms; warm cache int16: p95 18ms; int8 warm: p95 14ms; int4 warm: p95 12ms on optimized hw, but on older gen int4 actually rose to 20ms due to dequant overhead. so results vary by hardware.
[33:05] Aisha: that variability is exactly our fear. can Redwood produce per-hardware matrix for our planned instance types?
[33:18] Jordan: yes — we'll run matrix for the instance families you specified (you sent list in the intake form).

[35:00] Tom: about dashboards — we'd like a per-route view where we can see token-level latencies, batch sizes, cache_hit_rate, and model_version. is that ready-made?
[35:12] Sofia: we have starter dashboards: route-overview, token-heatmap, batch-distribution, and cost-estimates. we'll share JSONs you can import into Grafana. link: https://redwood.example.com/assets/zenith-grafana-starter.json

[36:30] Aisha: for pilot acceptance, success criteria: p95 latency under 250ms for our 128-token flows and cost per 1M tokens reduced by >=15% vs current provider. can Redwood help guarantee that?
[36:50] Mira: we can commit to a pilot SLA and measurable metrics. Dedicated with prefix cache + int8 quant for the right hardware likely meets that; we'll validate in the baseline run.

[38:20] Rajiv: procurement question — do you have pricing for Dedicated reserved capacity? also trial pricing for pilot?
[38:32] Mira: yes we'll send a draft pricing sheet. pilot often runs as discounted reserved two-week capacity or usage credits to cover benchmarking.

[40:00] (brief pause, someone drops connection for ~30s, reconnects)
[40:40] Tom: thanks for bearing — back now.

[41:10] Liam: one more technical detail — we emit a span per token-generation step only when token-level debug enabled; otherwise aggregate spans by generation stage to reduce load. enabling full per-token spans at high QPS will increase telemetry volume substantially.
[41:40] Lea: do you encrypt telemetry in transit?
[41:50] Mira: yes, OTLP over gRPC TLS is supported, and mTLS can be configured for Private/Dedicated peered setups.

[43:00] Aisha: about sampling again — if we sample traces we might miss rare cold-cache events. any trick?
[43:12] Liam: tail-based sampling, where you sample traces with high latency or error flags, captures those cold-cache tails. collector rules can do that.

[45:00] Mira: recap — we'll: 1) send OTEL collector config + Grafana JSON; 2) run baseline token-latency matrix (cold/warm, int16/int8/int4) on Dedicated; 3) provide security pack (SOC2, SAML examples, KMS notes); 4) schedule pilot start and exchange network details.

[45:35] Aisha: timeline — aim to start pilot week of 2025-04-21.
[45:42] Mira: noted.

[46:00] Rajiv: quick ask — can we get an example of the span names and attributes so our observability team can map them to existing dashboards?
[46:10] Liam: yes — example span names: redwood.request, redwood.runtime.loop, redwood.batch. attributes: model.family, model.version, pool_id, batch_id, token_index, token_latency_ms, cache_hit, request_id, user_tier. We'll paste exact JSON.

[47:20] Tom: do you support export to Tempo/Jaeger or only to OTLP?
[47:30] Liam: OTLP is the protocol; you can backends to Tempo, Jaeger, Lightstep, Datadog (via exporters). Prometheus metrics pipeline is separate.

[49:00] Sofia: also we'll include sample prom scrape configs and the prometheus metric names.

[50:10] Aisha: any known limitations with our stack — we use Grafana + Tempo + Prometheus (long-term metrics in Cortex).
[50:20] Liam: this is a common stack. just watch retention and sampling for traces (tempo storage can get large). metrics are tiny compared to full traces.

[51:00] (short pause)
[51:20] Mira: competitor check — you asked earlier: some customers compare our observability to Anthropic and OpenAI hosted endpoints; main diff is we give token-level control and integrated Optimize tooling.

[52:05] Rajiv: last sec security check — for pilot we need SAML config and SSO test accounts.
[52:18] Lea: also need data processing agreement for PHI — can you sign a DPA?
[52:25] Mira: yes we have standard DPA templates and enterprise addendums for PHI. we'll share legal contact.

[53:30] Liam: few more notes — when you run the baseline, include both synthetic high-QPS and production replay to capture non-uniform token distributions. KV cache gains are much larger in replay since prefixes repeat.

[54:00] Aisha: good — we'll export sample logs and prompts for you to run.

[55:30] Mira: final recap of next steps (verbal): I'll send collector configs, dashboard JSONs, pricing draft, and security pack. Liam will run the baseline and provide CSV + prometheus dump. Tom to send VPC peering details. We'll reconvene after baseline.

[56:05] (small talk)
[56:40] Rajiv: appreciate the level of detail. looking forward to results.

[58:00] Mira: ok thanks everyone — we'll follow up in email and schedule.
[58:05] (call ends)

Notes & references mentioned during call:
- Starter Grafana JSON: https://redwood.example.com/assets/zenith-grafana-starter.json

- Example dashboard gist: https://gist.github.com/redwood/zenith-otel-dashboard

[35:00] Tom: about dashboards — we'd like a per-route view where we can see token-level latencies, batch sizes, cache_hit_rate, and model_version. is that ready-made?
[35:12] Sofia: we have starter dashboards: route-overview, token-heatmap, batch-distribution, and cost-estimates. we'll share JSONs you can import into Grafana. link: https://redwood.example.com/assets/zenith-grafana-starter.json

[36:30] Aisha: for pilot acceptance, success criteria: p95 latency under 250ms for our 128-token flows and cost per 1M tokens reduced by >=15% vs current provider. can Redwood help guarantee that?
[36:50] Mira: we can commit to a pilot SLA and measurable metrics. Dedicated with prefix cache + int8 quant for the right hardware likely meets that; we'll validate in the baseline run.

[38:20] Rajiv: procurement question — do you have pricing for Dedicated reserved capacity? also trial pricing for pilot?
[38:32] Mira: yes we'll send a draft pricing sheet. pilot often runs as discounted reserved two-week capacity or usage credits to cover benchmarking.

[40:00] (brief pause, someone drops connection for ~30s, reconnects)
[40:40] Tom: thanks for bearing — back now.

[41:10] Liam: one more technical detail — we emit a span per token-generation step only when token-level debug enabled; otherwise aggregate spans by generation stage to reduce load. enabling full per-token spans at high QPS will increase telemetry volume substantially.
[41:40] Lea: do you encrypt telemetry in transit?
[41:50] Mira: yes, OTLP over gRPC TLS is supported, and mTLS can be configured for Private/Dedicated peered setups.

[43:00] Aisha: about sampling again — if we sample traces we might miss rare cold-cache events. any trick?
[43:12] Liam: tail-based sampling, where you sample traces with high latency or error flags, captures those cold-cache tails. collector rules can do that.

[45:00] Mira: recap — we'll: 1) send OTEL collector config + Grafana JSON; 2) run baseline token-latency matrix (cold/warm, int16/int8/int4) on Dedicated; 3) provide security pack (SOC2, SAML examples, KMS notes); 4) schedule pilot start and exchange network details.

[45:35] Aisha: timeline — aim to start pilot week of 2025-04-21.
[45:42] Mira: noted.

[46:00] Rajiv: quick ask — can we get an example of the span names and attributes so our observability team can map them to existing dashboards?
[46:10] Liam: yes — example span names: redwood.request, redwood.runtime.loop, redwood.batch. attributes: model.family, model.version, pool_id, batch_id, token_index, token_latency_ms, cache_hit, request_id, user_tier. We'll paste exact JSON.

[47:20] Tom: do you support export to Tempo/Jaeger or only to OTLP?
[47:30] Liam: OTLP is the protocol; you can backends to Tempo, Jaeger, Lightstep, Datadog (via exporters). Prometheus metrics pipeline is separate.

[49:00] Sofia: also we'll include sample prom scrape configs and the prometheus metric names.

[50:10] Aisha: any known limitations with our stack — we use Grafana + Tempo + Prometheus (long-term metrics in Cortex).
[50:20] Liam: this is a common stack. just watch retention and sampling for traces (tempo storage can get large). metrics are tiny compared to full traces.

[51:00] (short pause)
[51:20] Mira: competitor check — you asked earlier: some customers compare our observability to Anthropic and OpenAI hosted endpoints; main diff is we give token-level control and integrated Optimize tooling.

[52:05] Rajiv: last sec security check — for pilot we need SAML config and SSO test accounts.
[52:18] Lea: also need data processing agreement for PHI — can you sign a DPA?
[52:25] Mira: yes we have standard DPA templates and enterprise addendums for PHI. we'll share legal contact.

[53:30] Liam: few more notes — when you run the baseline, include both synthetic high-QPS and production replay to capture non-uniform token distributions. KV cache gains are much larger in replay since prefixes repeat.

[54:00] Aisha: good — we'll export sample logs and prompts for you to run.

[55:30] Mira: final recap of next steps (verbal): I'll send collector configs, dashboard JSONs, pricing draft, and security pack. Liam will run the baseline and provide CSV + prometheus dump. Tom to send VPC peering details. We'll reconvene after baseline.

[56:05] (small talk)
[56:40] Rajiv: appreciate the level of detail. looking forward to results.

[58:00] Mira: ok thanks everyone — we'll follow up in email and schedule.
[58:05] (call ends)

Notes & references mentioned during call:
- Starter Grafana JSON: https://redwood.example.com/assets/zenith-grafana-starter.json
- OTEL integration guide (to be shared): https://redwood.example.com/docs/otel-integration
- Example dashboard gist: https://gist.github.com/redwood/zenith-otel-dashboard
- Baseline spreadsheet (to be shared after run)

End of transcript.

title: POC roadmap: kickoff, access setup, midpoint review & exec readout (Redwood <> Aequitas Health)
preview:
Header:
Date: 2025-03-12
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~58 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Shah (SE), Evan Cole (Platform), Lisa Nguyen (Aequitas VP Product), Rahul Mehta (Head MLOps), Tom Alvarez (Security), Sana Ali (Data)

Summary:
Quick scoping call to align on POC timeline and success criteria for evaluating Redwood inference across Aequitas Health conversational and triage workloads. Primary constraints: HIPAA-adjacent security requirements, latency for clinician-facing UI, and cost per 1k tokens. Agreement to run a 4-week POC with clear milestones: kickoff, sandbox access & data onboarding, mid-point benchmark review, and exec readout.

Topics:
- POC timeline & milestones
- Access & security setup (VPC, KMS, SOC2)
- Workload sizing and benchmark profiles (seq len, concurrency, qps)
- Success criteria (latency, cost, functional parity)
- Reporting & exec readout expectations

Action items (recap): see metadata action_items above

Transcript body (excerpt, timestamps approximated, transcription imperfect in places):
00:00 - Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. We'll keep to 45-ish minutes then open for Qs, we're trying to get a POC timeline that works with your security process.
00:18 - Lisa: Yep, thanks. We're looking at this for clinician triage — must be responsive, low latency, and we need to be able to show execs that it won't regress quality vs current heuristic pipeline.
00:32 - Priya: Awesome. High level, our recommended POC is four weeks: kickoff, access/setup and data upload, mid-point review with benchmark results, then an exec readout. We can compress if you have hard deadlines but that's the comfortable cadence.
00:49 - Rahul: A couple constraints: we need any external platform to be able to integrate with our VPC or accept encrypted S3 + limited access. Also KMS keys need to be customer-managed — can't keep patient data in plaintext even in sandbox.
01:05 - Evan: Understood. We support Redwood Private for VPC/on-prem, and for hosted we can do a private control plane with customer-managed KMS; it's basically the same access patterns. We'll share the security checklist.
01:20 - Tom: Are you SOC2 Type II attested? We need attestation or a roadmap to attestation. Also encryption at rest and in transit, audit logs, and SSO/SAML with SCIM provisioning.
01:36 - Jordan: Yes, SOC2 present — we'll share the report. We also have RBAC, audit logs, and KMS integrations with major cloud KMS and HSM partners. I'll flag that Priya will send the security docs after this call.
01:51 - Lisa: On the technical success criteria — what metrics should we measure? Latency p50/p95/p99, throughput, token cost, and functional fidelity (classification/triage accuracy) versus our current model.
02:08 - Priya: Right. For latency we'd recommend target p99 < 500ms for interactive clinician flows with avg request length 200-400 tokens. For batch triage jobs we can accept higher. For cost, we'd set a target unit cost delta vs your current baseline — something like <= $0.0025 per token for the main gen model, but we can tune with quantization and batching.
02:29 - Rahul: Can you run that on Dedicated capacity? We want predictable tail latency during peak clinic hours — 9 to 12 and 14 to 17 local.
02:40 - Priya: Yes — Redwood Dedicated gives reserved GPU pools and predictable throughput. For the POC we'll usually run in the Redwood sandbox hosted account but configure a dedicated pool simulating your production load. We can also test failover to cheaper variants for degradation.
02:55 - Sana: For workload sizing, what's your ask? We have about 120 concurrent clinicians, but most sessions are bursty — average QPS per clinician is low, but aggregate peaks matter.
03:12 - Evan: For sizing we need: typical seq length distribution (min/avg/max tokens), concurrency during peak, percent of calls with streaming vs non-streaming responses, and the embedding vs generation split. Priya will share a sample sizing template.
03:34 - Lisa: Timeline concerns for exec: we want an internal board update week of April 21, so ideally exec readout in the week prior. That compresses timeline — can we finish sooner?
03:47 - Jordan: If we sign SOW this week and you can provide test data quickly, we can shift to a 3-week aggressive POC: kickoff week 0, access + onboarding week 1, mid-point week 2, exec readout week 3. But that's tight with your security review.
04:05 - Tom: Security review will take time; we can probably turn around the questionnaire in 2 business days, but provisioning S3 and customer KMS into your sandbox may need approvals. We'll aim to expedite.
04:20 - Priya: For benchmarks we'll propose three profiles: interactive clinician flow (200-400 tokens, target p99 < 500ms), rapid triage batch (avg 800 tokens, throughput-focused), and embeddings for retrieval (embedding throughput and recall). We'll measure accuracy on held-out labeled prompts you provide.
04:44 - Rahul: For the functional fidelity metric, we'll accept either token-level perplexity delta or end-task accuracy (F1) for triage categories. Which is easier for you?
04:58 - Priya: End-task accuracy/F1 is better for you since it maps to product KPIs. We'll run eval harness and produce a report with confusion matrices and suggestions if regression appears — e.g., change model variant or tweak temperature.
05:18 - Sana: Can Redwood run A/B comparisons against our current in-house model during the POC? We want apples-to-apples.
05:27 - Evan: Yes, we can support A/B at the routing layer or by parallel inference runs, capturing identical prompts and returning side-by-side outputs for downstream eval. We'll produce per-prompt latency and cost delta.
05:42 - Lisa: Mid-point review — what format? Internal stakeholders will want a quick slide deck, numbers, and a demo.
05:50 - Jordan: We'll do a 30-minute mid-point call with results snapshot, key graphs (latency distribution, cost per 1k tokens, accuracy vs baseline), and recommended next steps. Then final exec readout will be a 45-minute session for leadership with an executive summary and demo.
06:08 - Tom: About logs — do you retain request/response logs? We need auditability, but patient PHI must be scrubbed or we must ensure data doesn't persist in hosted logs.
06:20 - Priya: For sandbox POC we can configure no persistence or ephemeral logs. For Dedicated/Private we can route logs to your logging stack. We'll document retention and redaction options.
06:36 - Rahul: Benchmarks — what about cold-start vs warm KV cache? Our prompts include long context windows and follow-ups. We'll want to see p99 cold vs warm.
06:50 - Evan: Good point. We'll run cold-start scenarios and warm-cache scenarios, and supply KV cache hit rate metrics. Also include continuous batching impact.
07:05 - Sana: On sample size, how many examples do you need for reliable eval?
07:12 - Priya: For classification/triage we'd recommend 3-5k labeled examples for initial eval, with stratified sampling across categories and prompt styles. For embeddings retrieval, a few thousand queries with human eval is common.
07:28 - Lisa: Ok — risks: 1) security signoff slow, 2) dataset anonymization time, 3) cost overrun if we run many full-load benchmarks.
07:39 - Jordan: We can mitigate on 3) by capping test hours and using smaller representative samples for early runs. We'll propose a budget cap in the SOW.
07:50 - Priya: Also note model quantization and batching recommendations can lower cost by 30-60% in many cases; we'll include suggested config.
08:02 - Rahul: On instrumentation, we need token-level cost and per-route breakdowns so product owners can see cost drivers.
08:11 - Evan: Redwood Console provides per-route token breakdowns, p99 latency graphs, and tracing hooks. We'll include a short console walkthrough during the kickoff.
08:24 - Tom: Any external compliance certs for the private control plane? We run some workloads on-prem - is there an on-prem package?
08:33 - Jordan: Yes, Redwood Private supports customer-managed control plane with on-prem packages; we'll outline the architecture and what we need from your infra team.
08:45 - Lisa: Perfect. Let's talk deliverables timeline concretely.
08:51 - Jordan: Proposed milestone schedule in SOW:
 - Week 0 (kickoff): align goals, share access checklist, walk console
 - Days 1-7: security questionnaire, S3/KMS provisioning, upload sample dataset, sandbox account creation
 - Days 8-14: run initial benchmarks (interactive+embeddings), iterate on prompts/configs
 - Week 2 midpoint: 30-minute review with metrics + go/no-go adjustments
 - Days 15-21: deeper tuning, longer-run throughput tests, prepare exec deck
 - Week 3 or 4: exec readout (final results, recommendation)
09:30 - Lisa: If we compress to 3 weeks we combine some phases; acceptable if security comes back quickly.
09:39 - Priya: We can run parallel tasks: while security is in review, we can start internal benchmark runs on synthetic data to validate harness. That shortens wall-clock time.
09:50 - Sana: Regarding evaluation criteria for the exec readout, can you provide a one-pager showing pass/fail gating?
09:58 - Jordan: Yes — we'll include a simple gate: 1) Performance: p99 below threshold or matching current latency for clinician flows, 2) Accuracy: F1 within X% of baseline or better, 3) Cost: within approved delta or show path to optimize below threshold, 4) Security: checklist items met or remediation plan.
10:17 - Rahul: For the cost target, let's make it explicit: target average cost per generated token <= $0.0025 across peak profiles; otherwise we need clear ops for batching/quantization to meet it.
10:31 - Evan: We'll model cost using your waveform (concurrency/seq length) and provide sensitivity tables in the SOW.
10:40 - Tom: Last question: if we find regressions in the mid-point, what's the escalation path?
10:46 - Priya: We'll flag regressions early; options include tuning prompts, switching to a different model variant, enabling more aggressive quantization, or extending the tuning window. We'll capture triage steps in the report.
10:59 - Lisa: Ok, happy with that. Let's close with next steps.
11:05 - Jordan: Recap: I'll send SOW and SSO/security artifacts; Priya sends benchmark harness and sizing template; Rahul and Tom will move security/credentials; Sana will share sample prompts and 3-5k labeled examples. We'll aim to get kickoff scheduled next week pending SOW sign.
11:26 - Lisa: Thanks all.
11:28 - Jordan: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up in email with links and dates.

End of transcript excerpt.

References/Attachments mentioned:
- POC SOW (to be shared)
- Security questionnaire (link to be provided)
- Benchmark harness repo (example scripts)
- Sample sizing template (CSV)

Notes on transcription quality: several proper nouns (Redwood product names, KV cache, KMS) may be slightly off in automated transcript. Occasional speaker label drift and filler words included.

title: Orion Defense - Private deployment technical deep dive
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-02-13
Start: 1:30 PM ET
Duration: 52 min
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood), Hiro Tanaka (Redwood), Karen Voss (Orion), Miguel Santos (Orion), Elena Park (Orion)

Topics
- Offline install bundle
- What breaks when outbound is blocked
- Upgrade process in a closed enclave

Transcript (excerpt)
Jordan Blake: just to level set, when you say enclave, does the cluster have any way to reach public endpoints?
Miguel Santos: no. there is no outbound. no proxy. it's a closed network.
Hiro Tanaka: ok. that maps to what we call disconnected or air-gap installs.
Karen Voss: we try not to use air-gap as a term, but yes, it's isolated.
Jordan Blake: got it. so we'd need to provide an offline artifact bundle  container images, helm charts, and checksums.
Elena Park: and we will need a list of exact image digests for security sign-off.
Miguel Santos: also anything that phones home will be caught. even like update checks.
Hiro Tanaka: understood. telemetry can be disabled; updates would be manual by bundle.
Jordan Blake: for certs, do you allow public OCSP?
Miguel Santos: no, internal PKI only.
Karen Voss: we also can't do pulls from docker hub etc. everything must be mirrored internally.
Jordan Blake: ok. we'll follow up with a bundle definition and a checklist for a closed network install.

title: HelioHealth - Security review (EU residency & support access)
preview:
MEETING HEADER
Date: 2024-10-04
Time: 11:00 CEST
Duration: 38 min

Topics (auto)
- EU data residency
- Support access and auditing

Transcript
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Today is mainly residency and what our support process looks like.
[00:35] Nina Kovacs: Our requirement is EU only. We also need to know if your support team outside EU can see anything.
[01:20] Ethan Park: Serving and storage can be in EU. For support, we have engineers globally, so the right control is RBAC + break-glass with auditing.
[03:05] Lea Martin: If someone in APAC accesses logs, is that considered data leaving the EU?
